story_title: A V ery Busy T eenager
story_summary: A former high-school student recounts a busy four years filled with strong academics, extensive extracurricular involvement, health crises, and the choice of a college major influenced by an aptitude test.

context.time_period: high school years (recounted more than fifty years later)
context.stakes: academic success, career choice, health risks, and personal development

core_conflict: Balancing intense academic and extracurricular commitments, parental expectations, health crises, and making a consequential career/major decision.

decision_moments.trigger: Mother forbade me from playing it.
constraints: Risk of punishment or parental anger
options: Obey mother and focus on band
Secretly attend basketball practices and play
decision_moments.choice: Secretly joined practices and played in one game.
decision_moments.reasoning: I always liked basketball and wanted to play on the high school team.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Being caught and punished; missing band practice
decision_moments.outcome: Mother found out and went into a rage. That was it—the end of my basketball career!

decision_moments.trigger: During my senior year, I began to be concerned about my career and what my college major should be.
constraints: Uncertainty about music as a viable career
Need to choose a meaningful future
decision_moments.choice: Resolved to major in accounting and enrolled in my first course at the University of Southern Mississippi the next year.
decision_moments.reasoning: Among about twenty-five professions, the highest rating by a wide margin was a category called 'CPA/Owner'.
decision_moments.outcome: Chose accounting as career; later described choice as 'It was just my destiny!'

decision_moments.trigger: Then, whammo, a year later the other lung collapsed.
constraints: Trauma and fear from the first surgery
Significant surgical risk at the time
options: Agree to undergo the second surgery
decision_moments.choice: Eventually acquiesced and went under the knife again.
decision_moments.reasoning: Because of the trauma of the first surgery, I resisted the second one. However, I eventually acquiesced
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Significant medical/surgical risk and long recovery
decision_moments.outcome: It was a scary time for me, and full recovery took weeks.

decision_moments.trigger: I was the editor my senior year and persuading other students to write articles was always a chore.
options: Do the bulk of the work personally
decision_moments.choice: I did about 90 percent of the work required to publish the paper.
decision_moments.reasoning: Persuading other students to write articles was always a chore and getting them to meet deadlines was even harder.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Overwork and reinforcement of poor delegation habits
decision_moments.outcome: Learned little delegation; 'Throughout my later business career, I struggled ... taking on too much of the responsibility myself and failing to distribute the workload.'

principles.principle: High-quality teaching produces lasting advantage and prepares for future success.
principles.evidence: the quality of the education delivered was simply superb.

principles.principle: Extracurricular activities build skills, confidence, and social bonds.
principles.evidence: All sorts of activities outside the classroom supplemented the academic program.

principles.principle: Leaders must develop delegation skills rather than assuming excessive responsibility.
principles.evidence: Throughout my later business career, I struggled from time to time with leadership and management issues —taking on too much of the responsibility myself and failing to distribute the workload.

principles.principle: Adversity can be an opportunity to learn durable skills.
principles.evidence: While sick and recovering, I read about and ordered a newly published book by Harry Lorayne called How to Develop a Super Power Memory.

decision_heuristics.heuristic: When uncertain about career direction, take aptitude tests and consult mentors before deciding.
decision_heuristics.evidence: I talked to Mr. Harrison, the school superintendent, about it and he suggested I take an aptitude test.

decision_heuristics.heuristic: If volunteers won't contribute, be prepared to complete critical tasks yourself but recognize and plan to develop delegation skills later.
decision_heuristics.evidence: I did about 90 percent of the work required to publish the paper.

decision_heuristics.heuristic: Use recovery or downtime to practice and acquire skills that yield long-term benefits.
decision_heuristics.evidence: While sick and recovering, I read about and ordered a newly published book by Harry Lorayne called How to Develop a Super Power Memory.

decision_heuristics.heuristic: Heavily weigh objective professional assessments when they clearly favor one option.
decision_heuristics.evidence: Among about twenty-five professions, the highest rating by a wide margin was a category called "CPA/Owner".

leadership_patterns.risk_tolerance: Willing to accept personal and medical risks when necessary; moderately risk-taking in personal pursuits.
leadership_patterns.communication_style: Hands-on and action-oriented; attempts persuasion but often resorts to doing work directly.
leadership_patterns.accountability_posture: Highly self-accountable, tends to assume responsibility rather than delegate.
leadership_patterns.ethical_framing: Pragmatic and self-reflective; accepts guidance from trusted authorities and acknowledges personal faults.

quotes.quote: I did about 90 percent of the work required to publish the paper.
She simply put her foot down and forbade me from playing it.
I was quite a busy guy.
I was always a good student, partly because of a fundamental curiosity.
